text: Women cps_sample grouped low/medium/high SES (SES = 1, 2, 3) based household income. ask: much earnings inequality lies within SES tier vs tiers, changed since 1982? use ystat = "CV2" (squared coefficient variation, σ2/μ2\sigma^2/\mu^2) rather "Var" high-income tier doesn’t mechanically dominate decomposition. code: df <- subset(cps_sample,              age >= 18 & age <= 49 & !is.na(earnweekf) & earnweekf > 0)  causal <- ineqx(   y = "earnweekf", ystat = "CV2",   treat = "mother", post = "byear",   group = "edu",   time = "year10", ref = 1980,   formula_mu    = ~ mother * byear * factor(edu) * factor(year10) + age,   formula_sigma = ~ mother * byear * factor(edu) * factor(year10),   weights = "earnwtf",   se = "delta",   data = df ) #> GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = 3535424937  #> GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = 3534988768  #> GAMLSS-RS iteration 3: Global Deviance = 3534986071  #> GAMLSS-RS iteration 4: Global Deviance = 3534986056  #> GAMLSS-RS iteration 5: Global Deviance = 3534986056  #> GAMLSS-RS iteration 6: Global Deviance = 3534986056

title: Examples
text: motherhood effect’s contribution inequality changed since 1980? type = "shapley" displays three-channel decomposition range across six possible orderings:  compositional channel (Δπ\Delta_\pi) large negative — share childbearing-age women mothers dropped substantially since 1980, compositional shift reduces contribution motherhood overall inequality. Behavioral changes (Δβ+Δλ\Delta_\beta + \Delta_\lambda, motherhood’s per-group effects changed) smaller, pre-treatment shifts (Δμ+Δσ\Delta_\mu + \Delta_\sigma, baseline inequality changes) contribute modest negative term. thorough analysis add controls race, marital status, age splines, family size; model pre-trends formally; use bootstrap rather delta-method SEs. See ?ineqx, ?boot_config, Tutorial full toolbox.

what: Var, CV², or VL — which inequality measure should I use?
title: FAQ
text: ineqx() accepts ystat = "Var", "CV2", (descriptive ) "VL". default, prefer "CV2" groups different mean levels — without normalising squared mean, Var decomposition mechanically dominated whichever group highest mean. Use "Var" levels comparable across groups dollar-scale interpretation matters. Avoid "VL" substantive decomposition work: log transform non-linear, within- -group components VLV_L can move opposite direction within- -group changes income scale. ineqx() issues runtime warning whenever "VL" used. See Model structure page §4 detail.

what: What is the difference between descriptive and causal decomposition?
title: FAQ
text: run ineqx() function — dispatch whether treat supplied: Descriptive (treat): total inequality split within- -group components, components shifted time? Calls Western & Bloome (2009) decomposition. Causal (treat): given binary treatment, treatment effect inequality split? ineqx() fits GAMLSS model mean log-SD, decomposes implied effect VV CV2CV^2 vs within (heterogeneity vs covariance sub-components). descriptive case answers “unequal growing?”; causal case answers “inequality differ removed treatment?” See Examples .

title: FAQ
text: longitudinal decomposition splits change treatment effect inequality three channels (behavioral, compositional, pre-treatment). Computing channel requires sequential parameter-switching argument, size one channel depends order parameters switched. three parameter blocks 3!=63! = 6 orderings. Shapley averaging takes unweighted mean across six canonical fix path-dependence kind decomposition. type = "shapley" plot also displays range across orderings, can see channels robust choice sensitive.

what: When should I use se = "delta" vs se = boot_config()?
title: FAQ
text: se = "delta" (default): closed-form standard errors via delta method, evaluated GAMLSS coefficient vcov. Fast, resampling, available integrated estimation mode. Use unless reason . want CIs don’t rely normal-approximation, (b) blending user-supplied params model params (delta method supported ), (c) want CIs quantities aren’t covered closed-form gradients yet. Note small B (e.g. B = 10) percentile CIs noisy — bump B = 500+ production use. se = "none" / FALSE: skip SE computation entirely. Useful exploratory work just want point estimates fast.

what: How do I supply externally estimated parameters?
title: FAQ
text: Use ineqx_params(data = ...) wrap data.frame canonical columns (group, pi, mu0, sigma0, beta, lambda) ineqx_params object, pass ineqx(..., params = ..., se = "none"). right path estimates model doesn’t fit GAMLSS template — example 2SLS specification, coefficients copied published table. See ?ineqx_params schema Tutorial walk-.

what: What’s the difference between type = "treat.params" and type = "outcome.params"?
title: FAQ
text: plot model-implied parameters function group (time, longitudinal). difference parameters: treat.params plots β\beta λ\lambda — causal effects treatment group mean log-SD. One panel . outcome.params plots μ(0),μ(1)=μ(0)+β\mu(0), \mu(1) = \mu(0) + \beta σ(0),σ(1)=σ(0)eλ\sigma(0), \sigma(1) = \sigma(0) e^{\lambda} — predicted levels control treatment group. Useful showing underlying potential outcomes drive decomposition. accept ci = TRUE (delta method bootstrap, whichever available fitted object) style = "line"/"point".

what: My CIs look invisible — what should I check?
title: FAQ
text: common causes: Lowercase ci. plot argument ci = TRUE, CI = TRUE. R case-sensitive CI silently lands ..., plot uses default (ci = FALSE). Vertical scale. y-axis spans 0–3 SEs ~0.05, bands real percent panel — get easily missed small figure. Try style = "point" render error bars instead ribbons; bars usually salient small SEs. se set "none". ineqx() called se = "none" se = FALSE, SEs stored ci = TRUE falls silently (warns, depending plot type). Bootstrap tiny B. B = 10, percentile bands dominated noise — may straddle zero everywhere. Bump B ≥ 500.

what: I get an error about vcov not being available — what’s wrong?
title: FAQ
text: delta-method SE path needs GAMLSS coefficient variance-covariance matrix, ineqx() stores () se = "delta" used (b) model fitted via integrated path (.e., blended user-supplied params). Two fixes: used se = "none" fitted via blending: re-run se = boot_config(B = 500). wibe / treat.params / outcome.params plots fall back bootstrap percentile CIs automatically. used se = "delta" model failed converge vcov block singular: simplify formulas (formula_mu / formula_sigma) drop sparsely-populated cells. degenerate vcov usually points collinearity issue design matrix rather problem ineqx.

title: Model structure
text: Take vector YtY_t individual incomes time tt, vector GtG_t group memberships (Gt=jG_t = j j=1,…,Jj = 1,\dots,J mutually exclusive groups). variance YtY_t splits cleanly within- -group part: Vt=∑jπjtσjt2⏟within Wt+∑jπjt(μjt−μ‾t)2⏟Bt\begin{equation} V_t \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_j \pi_{jt}\,\sigma_{jt}^2}_{\text{within } W_t}        \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_j \pi_{jt}\,\big(\mu_{jt} - \bar\mu_t\big)^2}_{\text{} B_t} \tag{1} \end{equation} πjt\pi_{jt} share group jj, μjt\mu_{jt} mean, σjt2\sigma_{jt}^2 variance, μ‾t=∑jπjtμjt\bar\mu_t = \sum_j \pi_{jt}\mu_{jt} overall mean. Cross sections collapse single tt. repeated cross-sections, change Vt−Vt0V_t - V_{t_0} decomposes contributions changing means (Δμ)(\Delta_\mu), changing dispersions (Δσ)(\Delta_\sigma), changing group shares (Δπ)(\Delta_\pi), component split - within-group share. ineqx() returns treat supplied — essentially Western & Bloome (2009) decomposition Shapley-averaged path.

title: Model structure
text: Let D∈{0,1}D \\{0,1\} binary treatment, βj\beta_j causal effect group jj’s mean, λj\lambda_j causal effect group jj’s log-SD (treated SD σj(0)⋅eλj\sigma_j(0)\cdot e^{\lambda_j}). treatment effect inequality, τ=V[Y(1)∣D]−V[Y(0)∣D]\tau = V[Y(1)\mid D] - V[Y(0)\mid D], splits - within-group part, splits heterogeneity covariance term: τB=Varπ(β)⏟hetB+2Covπ(μ(0),β)⏟covBτW≈2σ‾2(0)λ‾⏟hetW+2Covπ(σ2(0),λ)⏟covW\begin{equation} \boxed{\;\tau_B \;=\; \underbrace{\mathrm{Var}_\pi(\beta)}_{\text{het}_B}             \;+\; \underbrace{2\,\mathrm{Cov}_\pi(\mu(0),\,\beta)}_{\text{cov}_B} \;} \qquad \boxed{\;\tau_W \;\approx\; \underbrace{2\,\bar\sigma^2(0)\,\bar\lambda}_{\text{het}_W}             \;+\; \underbrace{2\,\mathrm{Cov}_\pi(\sigma^2(0),\,\lambda)}_{\text{cov}_W}\;} \tag{2} \end{equation} four sub-components direct substantive readings: hetB=Varπ(β)≥0\text{het}_B = \mathrm{Var}_\pi(\beta) \ge 0. Heterogeneity treatment effect mean always widens -group inequality. Equal β\beta across groups means hetB=0\text{het}_B = 0. covB=2Covπ(μ(0),β)\text{cov}_B = 2\,\mathrm{Cov}_\pi(\mu(0),\beta). Sorting gains. positive, larger gains accrue already-advantaged groups (reinforces hierarchy). negative, treatment compresses hierarchy. hetW∝λ‾\text{het}_W \propto \bar\lambda. average effect within-group dispersion. Sign tracks λ‾\bar\lambda: treatment can stretch compress within-group spread. covW=2Covπ(σ2(0),λ)\text{cov}_W = 2\,\mathrm{Cov}_\pi(\sigma^2(0),\lambda). Sorting variance effects. positive, treatment amplifies dispersion already-disperse groups. plot(<causal>, type = "wibe", stats = c("het_b","cov_b","het_w","cov_w")) shows four series directly. package supports VV (variance) CV2=σ2/μ2CV^2 = \sigma^2/\mu^2 (squared coefficient variation) underlying inequality measure.

title: Model structure
text: repeated cross-sections, change treatment effect inequality, Δτ=τt−τt0\Delta\tau = \tau_t - \tau_{t_0}, decomposes three interpretable channels: Δτ=(Δβ+Δλ)⏟behavioral+(Δπ,B+Δπ,W)⏟compositional+(Δμ+Δσ)⏟pre-treatment\begin{equation} \Delta\tau \;=\; \underbrace{(\Delta_\beta + \Delta_\lambda)}_{\text{behavioral}} \;+\; \underbrace{(\Delta_{\pi,B} + \Delta_{\pi,W})}_{\text{compositional}} \;+\; \underbrace{(\Delta_\mu + \Delta_\sigma)}_{\text{pre-treatment}} \tag{3} \end{equation} Behavioral — changes treatment affects group’s mean variance (Δβ\Delta_\beta, Δλ\Delta_\lambda). Kitagawa-Blinder-Oaxaca “coefficient effect.” Compositional — changes gets treated across groups (Δπ,B,Δπ,W\Delta_{\pi,B}, \Delta_{\pi,W}). KBO “endowment effect.” Pre-treatment — changes baseline distribution determine much fixed treatment effect translates inequality (Δμ,Δσ\Delta_\mu, \Delta_\sigma). KBO analogue — variance-decomposition framing adds. Sequential decompositions kind path-dependent: size Δβ\Delta_\beta depends whether switch β\beta π\pi. ineqx() defaults order = "shapley", averages across 3!=63! = 6 orderings give order-invariant decomposition. plot(<causal>, type = "shapley") displays Shapley point estimate range across orderings.

title: Model structure
text: ineqx() accepts ystat = "VL" descriptive case (log-transforms yy runs standard variance decomposition), paper recommends VLV_L substantive decomposition work. reason: log transformation non-linear, within- -group components VLV_L can move opposite direction within- -group changes income scale. treatment unambiguously raises within-group dispersion dollars can register compressing VLV_L. VV CV2CV^2 pathology decompose components remain interpretable original income scale. ineqx() issues runtime warning whenever ystat = "VL" used — see FAQ details.

title: Getting Started with ineqx
text: simplest approach: pass formulas data directly ineqx(), fits GAMLSS model internally. Note formula_mu one-sided — outcome specified via y argument: default type = "wibe" shows within- -group treatment effects bar chart. show argument controls sub-components display (default: c("tau", "cov")):
code: result <- ineqx(   y             = "inc",   treat         = "x",   group         = "group",   formula_mu    = ~ x * factor(group),   formula_sigma = ~ x * factor(group),   se            = "delta",   data          = incdat ) #> GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = 16642.58  #> GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = 16642.58  print(result) #> Cross-sectional causal variance decomposition #> Inequality measure: Var  #> SEs via delta method #>  #> Treatment effect on outcome variance: #>     Var[Y | T = 0]:                          0.9791  (SE = 0.0681) #>     Var[Y | T = 1]:                        142.4776  (SE = 6.3270) #>     Total effect (tau_T):                  141.4984  (SE = 6.3274) #>       Between-group (tau_B):                17.4602  (SE = 2.6803) #>       Within-group (tau_W):                124.0382  (SE = 5.7317) #>       -> Wald test H0: lambda = 0: chi2(3) = 7364.6732, p < 0.0001 #>          Significant: evidence of treatment effect heterogeneity #>  #> Between-group sub-components: #>     Var_pi(beta):                           17.6956  (SE = 2.7254) #>     2*Cov_pi(mu0, beta):                    -0.2354  (SE = 0.3985) #>  #> Within-group sub-components: #>     mean(sigma0^2) * mean(f):              125.6536  (SE = 9.4712) #>     Cov_pi(sigma0^2, f):                    -1.6154  (SE = 7.4628) plot(result)

title: Getting Started with ineqx
text: Just descriptive decomposition supports counterfactual baseline, causal decomposition can blend user-provided counterfactual parameters model-estimated parameters data. answers questions like: “treatment effects inequality evolve relative hypothetical baseline?” Provide ineqx_params object (causal columns mu0, sigma0, beta, lambda) together treat, formula_mu, formula_sigma. function fits GAMLSS model data, blends model-estimated parameters counterfactual scenarios. can specify counterfactual parameters multiple time points. Periods covered params override model estimates, remaining periods estimated data. place two counterfactual scenarios — one beginning (time 0: treatment effect) one middle (time 3: moderate, uniform treatment effect) — data-estimated periods : Time 0 time 3 use counterfactual parameters; times 1, 2, 4, 5 estimated data via GAMLSS. longitudinal decomposition shows treatment effects inequality evolved across periods relative reference (time 0). Note: delta method SEs available blended parameters. Use boot_config() bootstrap SEs needed.

    sigma0 = 1,     # time 0: no treatment effect     # time 3: moderate uniform treatment effect     beta   = c(0, 0, 0, 5, 5, 5),     lambda = c(0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1)   ) )  result_blend <- ineqx(   y title: Getting Started with ineqx
text: want full control model specification (e.g., different distributions, random effects, custom controls), fit gamlss model pass ineqx_params():
code: library(gamlss) #> Warning: package 'gamlss' was built under R version 4.5.2 #> Warning: package 'gamlss.data' was built under R version 4.5.2 #> Warning: package 'gamlss.dist' was built under R version 4.5.2 #> Warning: package 'nlme' was built under R version 4.5.2  # Fit your own GAMLSS my_model <- gamlss(   formula       = inc ~ x * factor(group) + factor(t),   sigma.formula = ~ x * factor(group) + factor(t),   data          = incdat,   family        = NO(),   trace         = FALSE )  # Extract parameters params_ext <- ineqx_params(   model = my_model,   data  = incdat,   treat = "x",   group = "group",   vcov  = TRUE )  print(params_ext) #> ineqx_params object #>   Type: cross_sectional  #>   Inequality measure: Var  #>   Groups (3): 1, 2, 3 #>  #> Group-level parameters: #>    group     pi   mu0 sigma0   beta  lambda #>        1 0.4737 13.87  4.191 0.1415 -0.3171 #>        2 0.2632 13.82  4.373 0.3946  0.2629 #>        3 0.2632 13.84  4.230 0.6273  0.9472 #>  #> vcov: 12x12  # Decompose ineqx(y="inc", group = "group", params = params_ext, data = incdat) #> Cross-sectional causal variance decomposition #> Inequality measure: Var  #> SEs via delta method #>  #> Treatment effect on outcome variance: #>     Var[Y | T = 0]:                         18.0601  (SE = 1.2547) #>     Var[Y | T = 1]:                         44.2609  (SE = 2.0622) #>     Total effect (tau_T):                   26.2008  (SE = 2.4139) #>       Between-group (tau_B):                 0.0347  (SE = 0.0262) #>       Within-group (tau_W):                 26.1662  (SE = 2.4138) #>       -> Wald test H0: lambda = 0: chi2(3) = 501.2301, p < 0.0001 #>          Significant: evidence of treatment effect heterogeneity #>  #> Between-group sub-components: #>     Var_pi(beta):                            0.0412  (SE = 0.0397) #>     2*Cov_pi(mu0, beta):                    -0.0065  (SE = 0.0321) #>  #> Within-group sub-components: #>     mean(sigma0^2) * mean(f):               26.1136  (SE = 2.8073) #>     Cov_pi(sigma0^2, f):                     0.0526  (SE = 2.5861)

what: ineqx: Descriptive and causal variance decompositions
title: Descriptive and Causal Variance Decomposition
text: Inequality usually summarized one number — Gini, variance, 90/10 ratio. number hides much inequality lives groups (men vs women, college vs non-college, …) much lives within , hides treatment, policy, binary predictor moves either piece. ineqx package addresses . single cross-section implements classic Western & Bloome (2009) within/decomposition. repeated cross-sections tracks piece changes time. — part ’s new Rosche (2026) — extends decomposition causal potential-outcomes setting: splits effect binary treatment inequality -group effect, decomposed heterogeneity term (Varπ(β)\text{Var}_\pi(\beta) — treatment effect vary across groups?) covariance term (2Covπ(μ(0),β)2\,\text{Cov}_\pi(\mu(0),\beta) — larger gains accrue already-advantaged groups?), within-group effect, similarly split average effect group dispersion (hetW\text{het}_W) sorting variance effects across groups (covW\text{cov}_W). repeated cross-sections, change treatment effect decomposes three channels — behavioral (treatment effects changed), compositional (gets treated changed), pre-treatment (baseline distribution changed) — combining variance decomposition Kitagawa–Blinder–Oaxaca-style attribution. ineqx package can analyze: inequality splits single point time within- -group components, split changed time, attributing change shifting means, dispersions, group sizes, treatment moves inequality — beyond just mean — four sub-components , effect evolved time, separating behavioral, compositional, pre-treatment channels. package supports variance (VV) squared coefficient variation (CV2CV^2) underlying measure (accepts ystat = "VL" descriptive case, caveats — see FAQ). ineqx() looks like: See Model structure page math, Examples page calls walked end-- end, FAQ common questions.
code: data(cps_sample)  # Descriptive: how does CV² of women's earnings split into within/between SES? ineqx("earnweekf", ystat = "CV2", group = "SES",       time = "year", ref = 1982, weights = "earnwtf",       data = cps_sample)  # Causal: how does motherhood reshape that inequality? ineqx("earnweekf", ystat = "CV2",       treat = "mother", post = "byear",       group = "edu", time = "year10", ref = 1980,       formula_mu    = ~ mother * byear * factor(edu) * factor(year10) + age,       formula_sigma = ~ mother * byear * factor(edu) * factor(year10),       weights = "earnwtf", se = "delta",       data = subset(cps_sample, age >= 18 & age <= 49 & earnweekf > 0))

text:
parameter \(p \\{\beta, \lambda, \pi, \mu, \sigma\}\), result reports two split components: Delta_

_B Contribution switching p (t0 t) change \(\tau_B\) Delta_

_W Contribution switching p (t0 t) change \(\tau_W\) variance (ystat = "Var"), \(\tau_B\) depends \((\pi, \mu, \beta)\) \(\tau_W\) \((\pi, \sigma, \lambda)\), -diagonal split parts (Delta_beta_W, Delta_lambda_B, Delta_mu_W, Delta_sigma_B) exactly zero. CV\(^2\), \(\tau_B\) \(\tau_W\) share grand-mean denominator, five parameters can contribute sides; split components capture coupling exactly. construction, split components sum cross-sectional change: \(\sum_p \text{Delta\_

\_B} = \tau_B(t) - \tau_B(t_0)\) similarly W side, Delta_total equals \((\tau_B(t) + \tau_W(t)) - (\tau_B(t_0) + \tau_W(t_0))\). Aggregate parameter components (Delta_beta, Delta_lambda, Delta_mu, Delta_sigma) reported sum B W parts; V equal previous (single-side) values exactly. Compositional effects remain split Delta_pi_B Delta_pi_W.

title: Fit a GAMLSS model for variance decomposition — fit_ineqx_model
text: Users want control model specification fit gamlss model directly use ineqx_params extract decomposition parameters. GAMLSS framework models conditional mean conditional variance outcome distribution. mean equation captures treatment affects average outcomes within group. sigma equation captures treatment affects dispersion outcomes within group. simple difference estimator, model : $$\mu_{ij} = \alpha_j + \beta_{D,j} D_i + Z_i \gamma$$ $$\log(\sigma_{ij}) = \alpha_j^{(\sigma)} + \lambda_{D,j} D_i + Z_i \gamma^{(\sigma)}$$ estimator, add pre/post interactions: $$\mu_{ij} = \alpha_j + \beta_{D,j} D_i + \beta_P P_i + \beta_{DP,j} D_i P_i + Z_i \gamma$$

code: ineqx(   y = NULL,   ystat = "Var",   treat = NULL,   post = NULL,   group = NULL,   time = NULL,   ref = NULL,   order = "shapley",   formula_mu = NULL,   formula_sigma = NULL,   params = NULL,   weights = NULL,   se = "delta",   data = NULL,   ... )

title: Variance decomposition — ineqx
text: y Character, name outcome variable data. ystat Character, one "Var" (default), "CV2", "VL" (variance log; descriptive ). "VL" implemented log-transforming y running standard "Var" decomposition; output labelled "VL". treat Character, name treatment variable data. Coded 0/1. NULL, descriptive decomposition performed. post Character, pre/post indicator designs. NULL simple difference estimator. used integrated estimation mode. group Character, name grouping variable data. time Character, name time variable data. NULL, single cross-section assumed. ref Numeric, reference time period. Required longitudinal decomposition (descriptive causal). order Decomposition ordering. Either: "shapley" (default): averages across 6 possible orderings. descriptive: permutation c("mu", "sigma", "pi"). causal: permutation     c("behavioral", "compositional", "pretreatment"). relevant longitudinal data reference period. formula_mu One-sided formula mean equation (integrated estimation mode). E.g., ~ treat * group + controls. outcome y prepended internally. formula_sigma One-sided formula log-SD equation (integrated estimation mode). E.g., ~ treat * group + controls. params parameter object created ineqx_params. Either ineqx_desc_params (descriptive counterfactual reference) ineqx_params (causal decomposition). ineqx_params provided together treat, formula_mu, formula_sigma, blending mode used: params define counterfactual baseline, GAMLSS model fitted estimate parameters observed periods. See Details. weights Character, name weight variable data. NULL, equal weights used. se Standard error method. One : "delta" TRUE (default): delta method SEs.     causal, requires vcov params. descriptive, uses sampling-based     covariance. "none" FALSE: skip SE computation "boot": bootstrap SEs default settings (equivalent     se = boot_config()) boot_config object: bootstrap SEs custom     settings (causal ) data data.frame containing variables. manual descriptive mode (y = NULL), must contain columns group, pi, mu, sigma, optionally time column. ... Additional arguments passed gamlss::gamlss() integrated- blending-estimation paths. Useful bumping iteration limit saturated models (n.cyc = 100) selecting non-default family.

title: Variance decomposition — ineqx
text: descriptive decomposition: ineqx_desc object.   cross-sectional causal: ineqx_causal_cross object.   longitudinal causal (specific orderings Shapley):   ineqx_causal_longit object. Shapley results include additional   fields $shapley, $all_orderings, $ranges.

title: Variance decomposition — ineqx
text: five usage modes: Descriptive (raw data) Provide y omit treat     params. Decomposes total inequality within- -group     components. longitudinal data reference period,     decomposes change time contributions changing means,     dispersions, group composition. Descriptive (counterfactual reference) Provide y, data,     ineqx_desc_params object via params. params     define counterfactual baseline; observed group-level statistics     computed data. decomposition shows parameter     contributes change counterfactual observed inequality. Causal (integrated estimation) Provide treat, formula_mu,     formula_sigma, data. function fits GAMLSS model,     extracts parameters, performs causal decomposition. Causal (counterfactual reference) Provide ineqx_params     object via params together treat, formula_mu,     formula_sigma. params define counterfactual baseline     (e.g., zero treatment effects), GAMLSS model fitted estimate     parameters observed periods. two blended longitudinal     decomposition showing treatment effects changed baseline.     Delta method SEs available; use boot_config()     bootstrap SEs. Causal (externally estimated model) Provide ineqx_params     object via params (without treat/formulas). Use     ineqx_params create one manually fitted     gamlss model.

title: Create an ineqx_params object — ineqx_params
text: Constructs standardized parameter object serves interface model estimation variance decomposition. decouples two stages: users can construct ineqx_params estimation method (GAMLSS, Bayesian models, simulation, manual specification).

title: Create an ineqx_params object — ineqx_params
code: ineqx_params(   data,   model = NULL,   treat = NULL,   group = NULL,   time = NULL,   post = NULL,   ystat = "Var",   vcov = NULL,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Create an ineqx_params object — ineqx_params
text: data descriptive mode: data.frame columns group, pi, mu, sigma. causal manual mode: data.frame columns group, pi, mu0, sigma0, beta, lambda (optionally time). model mode: data.frame used fit model. model fitted gamlss object. provided, parameters extracted model via counterfactual predictions. Default: NULL (manual specification). treat Character, name treatment variable data. Required model provided. Must coded 0 (untreated) 1 (treated), multiple non-zero treatment levels. group Character, name grouping variable data. Required model provided. time Character, name time variable. NULL cross-sectional. Used manual mode (data contains time column) model mode. post Character, name pre/post indicator designs. used model mode. NULL simple difference estimator. ystat Character, either "Var" (variance) "CV2" (squared coefficient variation). Default: "Var". vcov manual mode: optional variance-covariance matrix (named list matrices longitudinal data). model mode: logical, whether extract vcov via numerical Jacobian (default: TRUE). Set FALSE faster computation without SEs.

title: Create an ineqx_params object — ineqx_params
text: object class "ineqx_desc_params" (descriptive mode)   "ineqx_params" (causal mode)

title: Create an ineqx_params object — ineqx_params
text: three usage modes: Descriptive (manual) Pass data.frame columns     group, pi, mu, sigma. Returns     ineqx_desc_params object representing counterfactual     reference descriptive decomposition. Causal (manual) Pass data.frame columns     group, pi, mu0, sigma0, beta,     lambda (optionally time). Returns     ineqx_params object. Causal (fitted gamlss) Pass fitted gamlss object     via model, along raw data variable names.     function extracts treatment effects via counterfactual predictions. mode auto-detected columns data: mu sigma present (without mu0, sigma0), descriptive path used; otherwise causal path used. model provided, function generates predictions counterfactual treatment assignments. observation, predicts outcome treat=0 (baseline) treat=1 (treated), mean (mu) standard deviation (sigma) equations. average marginal effects computed within group-time cell. simple difference estimator, ATT : $$\beta_{D,j} = E[\hat{\mu}(D=1) - \hat{\mu}(D=0) | G=j]$$

title: Create an ineqx_params object — ineqx_params
code: # Descriptive counterfactual reference (equal groups, no inequality) desc_ref <- ineqx_params(   data = data.frame(     group = c("workers", "managers"),     pi = c(0.5, 0.5),     mu = c(0, 0),     sigma = c(0, 0)   ) )  # Causal manual specification for a two-group example params <- ineqx_params(   data = data.frame(     group = c("workers", "managers"),     pi = c(0.5, 0.5),     mu0 = c(500, 1000),     sigma0 = c(200, 400),     beta = c(60, 100),     lambda = c(-0.1, -0.2)   ) )  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # From a fitted gamlss model params <- ineqx_params(   model = my_gamlss, data = mydata,   treat = "x", group = "SES",   ystat = "Var", vcov = TRUE ) } # }

title: Plot cross-sectional causal decomposition — plot.ineqx_causal_cross
text: x ineqx_causal_cross object type Character, plot type: "wibe" Within/treatment effects (bar chart). "wibe.group" Group-level contributions within/. "treat" Predicted treatment effect distributions group. "treat.params" Treatment effect parameters (beta, lambda)     group (bar chart). "outcome" Predicted outcome distributions (control vs     treated) group. Accepts FALSE "none" (CIs), TRUE "delta" (use stored SEs), "boot" (bootstrap defaults), boot_config() (bootstrap custom settings). Default FALSE. stats Character vector. type = "wibe": components display. bar plot recognizes canonical vocabulary longitudinal type = "wibe" method: "tau" (Total τ bar), "tau_b" (τ bar), "tau_w" (Within τ bar), "het_b", "cov_b", "rescale_b" (sub-components: heterogeneity, sorting, rescaling), within mirrors "het_w", "cov_w", "rescale_w". Single-segment bars allowed. legend labels "cov_*" “Sorting” "rescale_*" “Rescaling”. Shorthands: "het_cov" = four heterogeneity / sorting sub-components (rescaling), "het_cov_b" = c("het_b","cov_b"), "het_cov_w" = c("het_w","cov_w"), "subs" = six sub-components (het + cov + rescale, within), "subs_b" = c("het_b","cov_b","rescale_b"), "subs_w" = c("het_w","cov_w","rescale_w"). tau sub-component appear column, bars dodged. Default "tau" preserves classic three-bar (Total//Within) layout; c("tau","het_cov") preserves dodged classic layout. trim Numeric 0 1. distribution plots ("treat", "outcome"): quantile trim tails. title: ineqx 0.4.0
text: treat specified: descriptive decomposition (ineq()) treat specified params provided: causal decomposition ineq() removed — use ineqx() without treat instead y now separate argument (character, column name data); formula_mu now one-sided (like formula_sigma) boot_config() now requires y argument one-sided formula_mu Default order changed c("behavioral", "compositional", "pretreatment") "shapley" Argument order: y, ystat, treat, post, group, time, ref, order, formula_mu, formula_sigma, params, weights, se, data Renamed modes: “Mode ” → “integrated estimation”, “Mode B” → “externally estimated model”

title: ineqx 0.4.0
text: Fully parallel argument structure: descriptive causal paths share y, group, time, ref, ystat, order, data Public API reduced 2 main functions: ineqx() ineqx_params()

title: ineqx 0.3.0
text: Streamlined 3 user-facing functions: ineq(), ineqx(), ineqx_params() desc_decompose() replaced ineq() enhanced counterfactual decomposition extract_params() merged ineqx_params(model = ...) causal_decompose_cross(), causal_decompose_longit(), causal_shapley(), fit_ineqx_model() now internal ineqx() se_method/boot arguments replaced unified se argument: "delta" (default), "none", boot_config() object ineqx() longer returns ineqx_result wrapper; returns decomposition object directly

title: ineqx 0.3.0
text: ineq(): descriptive W/B decomposition counterfactual parameter-switching decomposition (mu, sigma, pi) Shapley averaging ineqx() integrated estimation: GAMLSS fitting via formula_mu/formula_sigma/data arguments order = "shapley" supported ordering-robust decomposition ineqx_params() accepts either manual data.frame fitted gamlss model (via model arg)

title: ineqx 0.3.0
text: Cleaner print/plot methods result classes

title: ineqx 0.2.0
text: Complete package redesign new modular architecture Old functions removed: wibe(), calcAME(), plot.ineqx() Replaced : desc_decompose(), extract_params(), new S3 plot methods c./. variable prefix convention removed; use plain column names zeallot dependency removed; gamlss moved Depends Suggests

title: ineqx 0.2.0
text: ineqx_params(): Standardized input format decouples estimation decomposition. Users can construct parameters estimation method. causal_decompose_cross(): Cross-sectional causal decomposition (treatment effect variance = within + ) causal_decompose_longit(): Longitudinal causal decomposition configurable decomposition ordering (6 possible orderings via order parameter) causal_shapley(): Shapley values averaged across 6 orderings robustness check desc_decompose(): Clean descriptive within/decomposition extract_params(): Extract decomposition parameters fitted GAMLSS models fit_ineqx_model(): Convenience wrapper fitting GAMLSS models Var (variance) CV2 (squared coefficient variation) supported throughout Print, summary, plot methods result classes
